kpi_cards.label: LME CASH ($/t)
kpi_cards.delta: 02-Jul Westmetall OFFICIAL cash $3,061.50 vs 01-Jul $3,075.00 = -$13.50 (-0.4%). The decline is decelerating (-1.25% on 01-Jul, -0.4% on 02-Jul after June's -1.7% legs) but price now sits below the $3,077 (.50 retrace) — ~21% below the 02-Jun four-year high ($3,855).

kpi_cards.label: LME 3-MONTH ($/t)
kpi_cards.delta: 02-Jul official $3,065.00 vs 01-Jul $3,073.00 (-$8.00, -0.3%). Two consecutive closes marginally below the .50 retracement ($3,077); momentum is slowing at support. Curve churned around flat: +$2.00 backwardation on 01-Jul, -$3.50 contango on 02-Jul.

kpi_cards.label: LME STOCK (t)
kpi_cards.delta: 02-Jul official 300,275 t (Westmetall) — a 1,500 t draw vs 01-Jul, now touching the ~300kt threshold flagged as the days-of-cover trigger. ~92% Russian-origin, ~1.3 days of global output. Steady draws persist through the price correction — the structural-deficit signal is intact.

benchmark.name: LME Cash settlement
benchmark.note: 02-Jul Westmetall OFFICIAL cash $3,061.50 vs 01-Jul $3,075.00 = -$13.50 (-0.4%). The correction is decelerating: -1.25% (01-Jul) then -0.4% (02-Jul) after June's -1.7% legs. Price sits just below the $3,077 (.50 retrace), ~21% below the 02-Jun four-year high ($3,855). avg = June-2026 final monthly mean ($3,458.6).

benchmark.name: LME 3-Month
benchmark.note: 02-Jul official $3,065.00 vs 01-Jul $3,073.00, -$8.00 (-0.3%). Two straight closes marginally under the .50 retracement ($3,077) — the war premium is ~fully unwound and the tape is testing whether this zone bases or breaks. avg = June-2026 mean ($3,438.9).

benchmark.name: Cash–3M spread
benchmark.note: 02-Jul official cash $3,061.50 vs 3M $3,065.00 = -$3.50 = shallow CONTANGO, after 01-Jul briefly printed +$2.00 BACKWARDATION — the front end is churning around flat as acute scarcity fears unwind while steady stock draws keep the nearby dates supported. No stress signal either way.

benchmark.name: SHFE front-month (excl VAT)
benchmark.note: SHFE aluminium extended lower post-holiday (SMM: futures continued to decline; A00 spot ¥24,370, -0.98% d/d), ~$3,055/t excl-VAT equivalent. The fall remains slower than the LME's, keeping the import-arb window shut; ingot social inventory fell another ~40kt w/w (SMM) as lower prices drew out buying — verify SMM/metal.com.

premiums.name: MJP Q2 (Main Japanese Port)
premiums.src: Quarterly settlement — FIXED within Q2 at an 11-yr high ($350–353, +79.5–81% q/q vs Q1 $195). Q3 negotiations still unsettled at 02-Jul; the Hormuz reopening and Gulf export resumption skew the pending range down toward ~$275–320 (S&P Global Platts MB-AL-0001).

premiums.name: CIF Japan spot
premiums.src: Spot CIF Japan continuing to drift lower into the Q3 reset as the LME slides and de-escalation holds — verify Fastmarkets MB-AL-0343.

premiums.name: European Duty Unpaid (EDU)
premiums.src: EU duty-unpaid in-whs Rotterdam; easing on weak demand but underpinned by the 6% duty + CBAM — verify Fastmarkets MB.

premiums.name: Billets Rotterdam Duty-Paid
premiums.src: Value-added conversion premium, in-whs dp Rotterdam — sticky vs ingot premia — verify Fastmarkets.

premiums.name: MW US Transaction (Platts)
premiums.src: Platts MW US Transaction premium; Section 232 (50%)-driven and structurally elevated, easing mechanically as the tariff-$ component shrinks on the lower LME (50% × LME) — verify PRA.

premiums.name: US Midwest Duty-Paid (P1020A)
premiums.src: Fastmarkets MB-AL-0020 ddp Midwest (≈$0.20–0.21/lb market premium + 50% tariff on a lower LME) — verify PRA.

macro.note: Fell to ~100.8 after June payrolls badly missed (+57k vs ~110k expected; Apr/May revised -74k) — on track to snap a two-week winning streak as Sep hike odds dropped to ~50% from ~64%. Dollar headwind for metals has eased (Trading Economics / BLS, 02–03 Jul).

macro.name: USD/JPY

macro.note: ~4.55 reference; the soft June payrolls print (+57k, u-rate 4.2%) trimmed Fed-hike odds (Sep ~50% from ~64%) — rate pressure on metals easing at the margin (re-validate post-holiday).

macro.name: Brent crude ($/bbl)
macro.note: Stabilised ~$70.5–71.7 (02-Jul) after the sub-$69 slide — UAE exports restored >3.9 mbpd and Hormuz flows >10 mbpd have unwound the war premium; the bounce reflects positioning, not fresh risk (Trading Economics / Forbes).

macro.note: Direct refining input; Gulf gas-disruption risk minimal with transit normalised.

macro_events: THE SLIDE IS DECELERATING AT KEY SUPPORT. The Westmetall OFFICIAL 02-Jul close ($3,061.50 cash / $3,065 3M) was only -0.3/-0.4% after -1.25% on 01-Jul — two closes now sit marginally below the $3,077 (.50 retrace), ~21% under the 02-Jun four-year high. The curve churned around flat (+$2.00 backwardation 01-Jul → -$3.50 contango 02-Jul).

US JUNE PAYROLLS MISSED BADLY: +57k vs ~110k expected (fewest in four months), u-rate 4.2%, Apr/May revised -74k (BLS, released 02-Jul ahead of the July-4 holiday). DXY fell to ~100.8 and September hike odds dropped to ~50% from ~64% — the dollar headwind on metals has eased; aluminium's spot reference ticked up ~0.3% to ~$3,095 on Friday (Trading Economics).

Strait of Hormuz / Gulf exports: transit is restored (crude >10 mbpd; UAE >3.9 mbpd) though 'patchy and not fully transparent' (Vanda Insights via Reuters). The market prices ~700kt of Gulf aluminium returning in H2 while EGA/Alba/Qatalum still run ~30–60% of capacity into Q4 — the last of the war premium is bleeding out against a still-impaired supply base.

China: ingot social inventory fell another ~40kt w/w (SMM) as lower prices drew out buying, but primary output keeps rising on new capacity/restarts and 2026 export projections stand at +12–18% (Fastmarkets) — the structural bearish counterweight.

Macro: the dollar firmed to DXY ~101.4 ahead of Friday's June payrolls, Brent stabilised ~$71 as Gulf barrels return, and dry-bulk freight turned up (BDI 2,650, +3.4%) — a mixed cost/currency backdrop with the Fed path the near-term swing factor.
lme_commentary: Fri 03-Jul evening run — the latest OFFICIAL close remains 02-Jul: $3,061.50 cash / $3,065 3M / stock 300,275 t (Westmetall); Friday's officials publish next session. The correction is decelerating right at the $3,077 (.50 retracement) — -1.25% (01-Jul), -0.3% (02-Jul) — and Friday's tape turned: the spot reference rose ~0.3% to ~$3,095 (Trading Economics) after US June payrolls badly missed (+57k vs ~110k; Apr/May -74k revisions), dropping the DXY to ~100.8 and September hike odds to ~50% — the dollar headwind has eased for the first time in the correction. The curve is flat (-$3.50 contango on 02-Jul after a brief +$2.00 backwardation) as acute scarcity fears unwind while steady stock draws (~1,500 t/day, ~92% Russian) support the nearby dates. Directional drivers unchanged: Hormuz transit restored (>10 mbpd; UAE >3.9 mbpd), ~700kt of Gulf metal priced to return in H2, China output/exports rising — against a Gulf supply base still at ~30–60% into Q4 and continuing China destocking. Two closes below $3,077 are not yet a confirmed break: a sustained break opens $2,950/$2,894 (.618); a reclaim of $3,260 (.382) is needed to stabilise.

alumina.name: Alumina FOB Australia (SMM/Fastmarkets)
alumina.src: ~$340/t FOB East Australia; Q2/H2 oversupply still pressuring — verify SMM/Fastmarkets

alumina.name: Alumina API / Platts region
alumina.src: Ex-China benchmark region; easing on the building surplus — verify Platts

alumina_notes: Alumina: ex-China FOB Australia (~$340/t) softer on Q2/H2 oversupply vs Platts API region (~$304/t; FOB futures ~$309.4); Nalco guides $320–330/t for 2026 as Indonesia/India refinery capacity ramps into subdued demand — a building ~1 Mt+ surplus.
Gulf alumina-logistics risk has largely cleared as Hormuz transit normalises (flows >10 mbpd) — ~60% of regional alumina imports transit the Strait, and flows have rebuilt on de-escalation.

Bauxite: Guinea's oversupply keeps CIF China ~$68/dmt (FOB Guinea ~$38, 45/3 grade); Guinea weighing export curbs (a >$100/dmt target) — a structural upside risk to raw-material cost independent of the Gulf.
Refining: Gulf gas-supply risk minimal with the strait open; EGA's Al Taweelah refinery restart timeline (~12 months) remains the key supply swing factor.
Net: alumina balance skews to surplus (ample supply caps the price); de-escalation removes the logistics overlay, leaving Guinea bauxite-curb risk as the main floor under raw-material cost.
net_read: WAR PREMIUM ~FULLY UNWOUND — THE DECLINE IS DECELERATING AT THE .50 RETRACE. Aluminium's run to a four-year high ($3,855 cash, 02-Jun) was built on the Gulf supply shock; the subsequent correction has now retraced half the advance. The Westmetall OFFICIAL 02-Jul close ($3,061.50 cash / $3,065 3M) was just -0.3/-0.4% after -1.25% on 01-Jul — a decelerating slide that leaves two closes marginally below the $3,077 (.50) support. The curve is churning around flat (brief +$2 backwardation on 01-Jul, -$3.50 contango on 02-Jul). Bearish forces: restored Hormuz transit (>10 mbpd, UAE >3.9 mbpd), ~700kt of Gulf metal priced to return in H2, rising China output and +12–18% export projections, though the dollar headwind just eased (DXY ~100.8 after the +57k June payrolls miss). The floor: Gulf capacity still ~30–60% into Q4, LME stock down to 300,275 t (~92% Russian) with steady ~1,500 t/day draws, and China ingot destocking continuing (-40kt w/w). US premiums stay tariff-inflated under the 50% Section 232 rate but ease mechanically with the LME; Asian premiums drift into the pending Q3 MJP reset (~$275–320).

feed.text: US June payrolls +57k vs ~110k expected, u-rate 4.2%, -74k back revisions (BLS) — DXY slid to ~100.8, Sep hike odds to ~50%; aluminium's Friday spot reference stabilised +0.3% at ~$3,095 (Trading Economics). Friday's LME officials publish next session.

Full 02-Jul base-metals officials posted (Westmetall): Cu 3M $13,250 (+0.3%) the only gainer; Ni $16,225 (-1.0%, 2026 lows), Zn $3,465 (-1.3%), Pb $1,870 (-0.4%) — the softness is broad, aluminium's stabilisation is the outlier.

Westmetall posted the 02-Jul OFFICIAL: cash $3,061.50 / 3M $3,065.00 / stock 300,275 t — a second, smaller down-day (-0.3%) leaving price marginally below the $3,077 (.50) support; the slide is decelerating.
Curve churn: 01-Jul printed a small +$2.00 BACKWARDATION before 02-Jul settled at a shallow -$3.50 contango — the front end is flat-lining as scarcity fears unwind against steady stock draws.
BDI jumped +3.4% to 2,650 (2-week high; capesize +6.2% to 3,291) on Australia/Brazil ore activity — dry-bulk costs turning up off the cycle low.
Brent stabilised ~$71 after the sub-$69 slide as UAE exports were restored to >3.9 mbpd and total Hormuz flows held >10 mbpd; DXY firmed to ~101.4 ahead of Friday's June payrolls.

Reuters half-year review: LME metals 'whipsawed by war and peace' in H1; with Hormuz reopening, analysts frame ~700kt of Gulf aluminium flowing back into the market in H2 — the key bearish swing for the second half.
SMM: China ingot social inventory fell another ~40kt w/w as the lower price drew out downstream buying; primary output still rising on new capacity/restarts — destocking cushions, supply caps.

news.headline: June payrolls missed at +57k (vs ~110k expected; Apr/May revised -74k; u-rate 4.2%) — the dollar index fell to ~100.8, on track to snap a two-week win streak, and September hike odds dropped to ~50% from ~64%. First supportive macro impulse for $-priced metals since the June correction began; aluminium's spot reference rose ~0.3% Friday to ~$3,095.
news.source: BLS Employment Situation (02-Jul) — bls.gov; Trading Economics — tradingeconomics.com

news.headline: Aluminium's slide decelerated: the Westmetall OFFICIAL 02-Jul close ($3,061.50 cash / $3,065 3M) was -0.3/-0.4% after -1.25% on 01-Jul — two closes now sit marginally below the $3,077 (.50 retracement), ~21% below the 02-Jun four-year high.

The cash–3M curve churned around flat: a small +$2.00 backwardation on 01-Jul, then a shallow -$3.50 contango on 02-Jul — acute scarcity fears are gone, but steady stock draws keep the front end supported.

De-escalation holds: Hormuz crude flows >10 mbpd with UAE exports restored >3.9 mbpd; Brent stabilised ~$71. Reopening remains 'patchy, unpredictable and not fully transparent' (Vanda Insights) — residual two-sided headline risk.

With Hormuz reopening, ~700,000 t of Gulf aluminium is framed to flow back into the market in H2-2026 — while EGA/Alba/Qatalum still run ~30–60% of capacity with full recovery unlikely before Q4: the supply floor erodes gradually, not suddenly.
China primary output keeps rising on new capacity and restarts; 2026 export projections stand at +12–18% (potentially ~7.2 Mt) — the growing structural counterweight to the Gulf deficit.
China ingot social inventory fell another ~40kt w/w as lower prices drew out downstream buying — destocking continues to cushion the downside despite soft sentiment.

Section 232 aluminium tariff holds at 50% on full customs value (June-2026 proclamation, through 2027; derivative carve-outs don't affect unwrought) — US MW ~$2,405/t stays structurally elevated but eases with the LME; Q3 MJP still pending (~$275–320).
news.source: White House / CBP; S&P Global Platts

news.headline: BDI jumped +3.4% to 2,650 on 02-Jul (capesize +6.2% to 3,291), a 2-week high off the cycle low — bulk freight for bauxite/alumina turning up at the margin.

earnings.readthrough: Higher Al prices + operational resilience amid Gulf disruption; San Ciprián smelter restart completed Apr 2026. Q2 report due ~mid-Jul.

earnings.headline: Adj. EBITDA NOK 8,668m (vs 9,516m yr-ago); adj. EPS NOK 2.07 (beat, up from 1.63).
earnings.readthrough: Primary Al EBITDA +2.7% YoY as Norwegian smelters reopened; soft alumina prices & stronger NOK; Extrusions outlook cut. Q2 due late Jul.

earnings.headline: Adj. EPS $1.63 (beat $1.56); revenue $649m; net income $327m (incl. $288m Hawesville sale gain).
earnings.readthrough: Higher LME + strong US Midwest premiums; Q2 adj. EBITDA guided $315–335m.

earnings.headline: Net profit $200.3m (+316% YoY); EPS 53 fils; sales −17% on Gulf supply-chain disruption.
earnings.readthrough: LME averaged $3,195/t (+22% YoY) drove profit surge; volumes hit by regional conflict; ~19% capacity in controlled shutdown — restart path improves post-truce.

earnings.headline: Net profit SAR 1.63bn (~$436m, +5.5% YoY); revenue SAR 8.79bn (+3.2%); EBITDA $964m.
earnings.readthrough: Strong pricing across commodities; partly offset by lower phosphate volumes; 2026 capex set >$4bn.

earnings.readthrough: Flags Gulf curtailments = 23% of ex-China supply; expects enhanced 2026 deficit; Kitimat/AP60 ramp-up.

earnings.readthrough: Alumina sales −15% q/q on availability/timing; annual guidance intact despite cyclone gas disruption.

earnings.readthrough: Record India operations; Novelis pressured by Oswego outage (~$104m) and tariffs (~$143m).

earnings.readthrough: Strong on higher Al prices; China output near self-imposed 45 Mt capacity cap.

earnings.headline: Underlying profit $6.2bn (+>20%); underlying EBITDA $15.5bn (+25%, 58% margin); interim div 73c.
earnings.readthrough: Copper-led strength; broad base-metals read-across positive for the complex.

earnings.headline: FY25 results; copper & aluminium average prices +9% YoY; zinc metal +4%.
earnings.readthrough: Resilient structural demand for Cu/Al cited; broadly constructive sector tone.

earnings.headline: FY25 net loss $455m (vs $803m profit in 2024) on higher costs & lower output.
earnings.readthrough: Analysts expect stronger H1 2026 on higher prices; Asia pivot amid fractured trade flows.

earnings_note: Earnings are quarterly/episodic; entries show each peer's most recent reported results as of 03-Jul (no new releases in the last 48h). Q2 season begins mid/late-July: Alcoa ~22-Jul (after close); Norsk Hydro late-Jul (large share of 2026 primary output and premiums hedged); Maaden (1211) late-Jul/early-Aug. Sources: company releases & SEC/exchange filings, Alcircle, Discovery Alert, Investing.com.

commercial.text: The slide is decelerating at the .50 retrace ($3,077) but hasn't confirmed a base: the 02-Jul official closed $3,065 3M with the curve flat (-$3.50). Keep LME hedges firmly in place on priced volume; pace unpriced tonnes rather than chasing weakness, and defend value-added (billet/slab/PFA) premium capture — conversion premia are stickier than ingot premia in a premium-unwind phase.

Asian premia keep drifting into the Q3 MJP reset (CIF Japan ~$338; pending Q3 range ~$275–320 vs Q2's fixed $351.5). Lock remaining elevated premiums on renewals before the reset, while Gulf capacity stays impaired. US MW ~$2,405/t remains structurally high on the 50% Section 232 rate but eases mechanically with the LME; EU underpinned by the 6% duty + CBAM.

Ex-China FOB Australia ~$340/t (API ~$304) with the Q2/H2 surplus capping upside; Nalco guides $320–330 for 2026. If net seller, transact into any firmness promptly; if buyer for smelting, the surplus offers progressively softer windows — a favourable input-cost backdrop.

Normalise routing as Hormuz transit runs >10 mbpd and Gulf exports resume, but note dry-bulk costs turned up (BDI 2,650, +3.4%). Keep war-risk cover and demurrage clauses live while Iran contests maritime control; hold the Jeddah/Red Sea land-bridge on standby until volumes fully recover.

Manage the turn with strategic accounts: the market narrative has shifted from supply-scare to H2 re-supply (~700kt Gulf return framing). Reaffirm reliability and OTIF, keep allocation transparent, and use dependability through the volatility to defend premium tolerance as buyers press for discounts into the Q3 resets.

Track: (1) whether closes below $3,077 (.50) stick — sustained break opens $2,950/$2,894 (.618); reclaim of $3,260 (.382) to stabilise; (2) Friday's US June payrolls / DXY ~101.4; (3) the pace of Gulf export resumption vs the ~700kt H2 framing and EGA/ALBA restart timelines; (4) Q3 MJP settlement (~$275–320 pending); (5) China export pace (+12–18%), output ramp and destocking; (6) BDI trend off the low.

bottom_line: On 03-Jul the correction is decelerating but not yet based. The Westmetall OFFICIAL 02-Jul close ($3,061.50 cash / $3,065 3M) posted a second, much smaller down-day, leaving two closes marginally below the $3,077 (.50 retracement) with the curve flat (-$3.50 contango after a brief +$2 backwardation). The war premium is ~fully unwound: Hormuz transit is restored (>10 mbpd; UAE >3.9 mbpd), ~700kt of Gulf metal is framed to return in H2, and China supply keeps rising — while the structural cushion (Gulf capacity ~30–60% into Q4, LME stock at the ~300kt threshold with steady draws, China destocking -40kt w/w) still argues the $2,950–3,080 zone is support, not air. US premiums stay tariff-inflated under the 50% Section 232 rate; Asian premiums drift into the pending Q3 MJP reset. Net: a decelerating downtrend testing key support, with Friday's US payrolls and the Q3 MJP settlement the next catalysts. Macro update: the June payrolls miss (+57k vs ~110k; DXY ~100.8, Sep hike odds ~50%) eased the dollar headwind, and Friday's spot reference stabilised +0.3% at ~$3,095 — a basing attempt at $3,077 is underway but unconfirmed until officials post next session.

Copper and nickel Westmetall pages had not posted 01/02-Jul officials at run time — the board carries their latest posted officials (30-Jun) with day-moves vs 29-Jun; zinc is 01-Jul and lead 02-Jul.

Live intraday CFD/FX feeds are indicative; macro values (DXY ~101.4, Brent ~$71, gold ~$4,010) are latest public references and should be re-validated at the London reopen. US June payrolls (Fri) is the near-term macro event.

PRA premiums (CIF Japan, EDU, billets, US MW) are public references with small directional drift applied per PRA commentary — verify against S&P Global Platts / Fastmarkets subscriptions before commercial use.

Hormuz status: transit restored (>10 mbpd; UAE >3.9 mbpd) but 'patchy and not fully transparent' (Vanda Insights) and Iran still contests maritime administrative control — two-sided headline risk persists.
Q3 MJP premium remains under negotiation (pending range ~$275–320); Q2 stays the last settled quarter ($350–353).

forward_path.basis: 02-Jul Westmetall official cash; -0.4% vs 01-Jul — decelerating slide, sitting just below the $3,077 (.50) support.
02-Jul Westmetall official 3M; -0.3% vs 01-Jul; curve flat (-$3.50 contango after a brief +$2.00 backwardation on 01-Jul).

outlook.curve_note: The front end is churning around flat — 01-Jul printed a small +$2.00 backwardation before 02-Jul settled at a shallow -$3.50 contango — as acute scarcity fears unwind against steady ~1,500 t/day stock draws (300,275 t, at the ~300kt threshold). Consensus 2026 average sits in the $3,200–3,500 band before easing into 2027 as Gulf supply is repaired; with ~700kt of Gulf metal framed to return in H2 and China exports running +12–18%, the structural deficit (impaired Gulf capacity into Q4, China destocking) is the floor under the $2,950–3,080 support zone.

consensus.note: Running above the World Bank base
First structural deficit in ~20 years; BofA flags near-term downside

balance.label: BofA/Morgan/BNP structural deficit; Goldman flags later surplus risk

capacity_pipeline.change: Output near the 45 Mt self-imposed cap; exports seen +12–18% (Fastmarkets), potentially ~7.2 Mt
capacity_pipeline.timing: Structural ceiling on output; rising exports

capacity_pipeline.change: New smelter capacity ramping (BofA flags as added supply)

scenarios.drivers: Gulf re-escalation / Hormuz re-closure, EGA/ALBA restart slips, deficit deepens, China stays capped
drivers_list: Geopolitics: ceasefire breaks down or Hormuz is credibly re-closed — war premium re-ignites
Supply: Gulf restart slips — EGA Al Taweelah / ALBA recovery runs beyond ~12 months, or fresh outages
Inventory: visible LME stocks draw below ~300kt — days-of-cover critical
China: output stays capped at 45 Mt; net exports fall as domestic demand absorbs metal
Targets: Citi/Goldman ~$3,600, upside ~$4,000+ only if the Strait stays shut beyond ~30 days

scenarios.drivers: Ceasefire holds; structural deficit floors price against China/Indonesia supply — elevated but drifting lower
drivers_list: Geopolitics: ceasefire/Hormuz reopening holds — no corridor risk premium
Supply: structural deficit persists (~-365 kt 2026) with Gulf capacity repairing gradually (the floor)
China: exports strong (~+12–18% YoY); output near the 45 Mt cap — the bearish counterweight
Macro: USD firm after the hawkish June FOMC; soft China demand caps rebounds
Curve: contango holds; price oscillates $3,100–3,450 around the support band

scenarios.drivers: Durable de-escalation, China + Indonesia supply surge, hawkish Fed/strong USD, demand softening
drivers_list: Geopolitics: full normalisation — Gulf restarts beat plan, no premium
Supply: China export surge plus rising Indonesian smelter output; Middle East inventories released
Macro: hawkish Fed / stronger USD; rate-hike risk weighs on dollar-priced metals
Demand: global manufacturing & construction soften; destocking sets in
Inventory: visible stocks rebuild as supply returns; contango deepens

risks.risk: China + Indonesia supply (export pace, 45 Mt cap)

catalysts.event: China June trade & aluminium export data
Alcoa Q2 earnings (after close) — sector kickoff

ai_analysis: What changed: the Westmetall OFFICIAL 30-Jun close ($3,105.50 cash / $3,112 3M) posted a fresh -1.7% leg down — the one-session base broke and price extended through the $3,140 shelf into the lower half of the $3,000–$3,170 correction zone.
Term structure: the cash-3M contango edged wider to -$6.50 (from -$5.50) as the front-end softened with the tape — consistent with the geopolitical premium continuing to unwind.

Drivers: de-escalation deepened (Brent <$69, Hormuz flows >10 mbpd, US–Iran talks in Qatar) while China primary output rebounded and 2026 export projections were lifted (+12–18%) — the bearish counterweights. The floor holds: EGA/Alba/Qatalum ~30–60% to Q4, China destocking at a ~4-yr weekly high, LME stock at 303,675 t.

Net base case: price tests the $3,000–$3,080 (.50) support; a sustained break opens $2,950/$2,894 (.618), while a reclaim of $3,260 (.382) is needed to stabilise. A fresh Gulf re-escalation is the main upside catalyst.

premium_explainer.desc: Quarterly-negotiated premium over LME cash for primary metal delivered to Japan — the Asian benchmark.
Spot premium for cargoes delivered cost-insurance-freight to Japan; leads the next MJP negotiation.
Premium for non-EU-origin metal in-warehouse Rotterdam before the 3% import duty — the European spot gauge.
Value-added premium for extrusion billet (conversion + logistics) on top of the ingot premium.
Delivered-Midwest premium over LME; heavily driven by US Section 232 tariffs and import economics.

premium_outlook: Asian (MJP / CIF Japan): Q2 MJP stays fixed near an 11-yr high (~$351.5/t); CIF Japan spot keeps drifting (~$338) with the LME slide. Q3 negotiations remain unsettled with the Gulf export-resumption signal skewing the settlement toward ~$275–320 (Platts/Fastmarkets).
European (EDU): ~$470/t, softer on weak demand but underpinned by the 6% duty + CBAM — the policy floor holds even as spot eases.
US Midwest: MW US Transaction ~$2,405/t; duty-paid P1020A ~$2,040/t — structurally elevated on the 50% Section 232 tariff, easing mechanically as the tariff-$ (50% × LME) shrinks on the lower LME.

Common thread: premiums spiked on the Gulf outage and keep easing as Hormuz transit normalises (>10 mbpd) and ~700kt of Gulf metal is priced to return in H2 — unrepaired Gulf capacity keeps a floor while the tariff/CBAM overlay holds US/EU premiums structurally high.

alumina_explainer.desc: Refined intermediate from bauxite via the Bayer process; ~1.9–2 t alumina per 1 t aluminium. Smelter-grade is the traded form.
The ex-China benchmark (Platts Alumina Price Index) referenced in many alumina contracts.
Spot index for Australian-origin smelter-grade alumina — a key seaborne reference.
The ore refined into alumina; ~2–3 t bauxite per 1 t alumina. Guinea & Australia dominate seaborne supply.
Primary Bayer reagent that dissolves alumina from bauxite — a major refining cost (~$80–120 per t of alumina).
Used for causticisation & desilication in refining — a smaller but essential reagent cost.

raw_materials.name: Bauxite (Guinea, CIF China)
raw_materials.src: ~$68 CIF China (FOB Guinea ~$38, 45/3 grade); oversupply has roughly halved prices since Jan-25 — Guinea weighing export curbs toward a >$100/dmt target (SMM/Fastmarkets/Discovery Alert)

raw_materials.name: Caustic soda (flakes ≥98%, FOB NE Asia)
raw_materials.src: ~$500–510 FOB NE Asia; CFR Asia ~$380–560 — major refining reagent (ChemAnalyst / ChemTradeAsia)

raw_materials.name: Lime / quicklime (CaO)
raw_materials.src: No liquid global index — largely regional/contract; indicative range (causticisation reagent)

alumina_outlook: Alumina: ex-China FOB Australia (~$340/t) easing on Q2/H2 oversupply; API region ~$304 (FOB futures ~$309). Surplus building into H2; the Hormuz reopening removes the Gulf-logistics risk overlay.
Bauxite: Guinea oversupply keeps CIF China ~$68/dmt (FOB Guinea ~$38) — Guinea's export-curb push (targeting >$100/dmt) is the key upside risk to raw-material cost.
Caustic soda firm (~$500/t FOB NE Asia) — a sticky refining cost; lime stable. Net refining-cost pressure eases with alumina/bauxite while caustic keeps a floor.
Read-through: softer alumina + bauxite lower a smelter's input cost; for an alumina seller, oversupply caps realizations into Q3 with Gulf logistics risk now receding.

inputs.name: Calcined petroleum coke (CPC, anode-grade)
inputs.src: Peaked ~$896/t (2022); battery-anode demand + tight seaborne supply pressuring — CRU; limited price transparency

inputs.name: Coal tar pitch (CTP)
inputs.src: Anode binder; by-product of steel coke ovens — supply tied to steel output; thin spot market

inputs.name: Pre-baked anodes
inputs.src: Often produced in-house; China the swing exporter — mostly contract pricing

inputs.name: Electricity (smelter power)
inputs.src: Single largest cost & the competitiveness driver; no single index — PPAs / regional grid / self-gen; rising carbon-cost exposure

inputs.name: Cryolite / bath makeup
inputs.src: Largely synthetic & recycled in-cell; limited spot market

inputs.name: Cathode / graphite blocks
inputs.src: Multi-year pot-reline cost; graphite-electrode supply (China) is the risk

inputs.name: Silicon metal
inputs.src: China Q2-26 ~$1,250 FOB; EU ~$1,580, US ~$2,625 (tariff split) — SMM/ChemAnalyst

inputs.name: Magnesium (99.9%)
inputs.src: China ~85% of global supply (~$2,359 Jan-26) — concentration risk; SMM/Asian Metal

inputs.name: Copper (LME)
inputs.src: LME-traded — full transparency (3M ~$13,366/t, ~$6.06/lb, 30-Jun official, steady on firm China data; COMEX near records on US tariff premium); alloy input for 2xxx & some 6xxx

inputs.name: Ti-B grain refiner (AlTi5B1)
inputs.src: Master-alloy rod; priced off titanium + conversion — mostly contract

logistics.name: Baltic Dry Index (BDI)
logistics.note: Bulk-freight proxy for bauxite/alumina; jumped +3.4% on 02-Jul to 2,650 — a 2-week high, third straight rise — with capesize +6.2% to 3,291 on improved Australia/Brazil ore cargo activity (Trading Economics / Baltic Exchange).

logistics.name: Strait of Hormuz transit
logistics.val: Open — crude flows >10 mbpd; UAE exports >3.9 mbpd
logistics.note: ~80–85% of Gulf Al exports & ~60% of regional alumina imports transit Hormuz. Flows are restored (UAE >3.9 mbpd; total >10 mbpd) and the market prices ~700kt of Gulf aluminium returning in H2. Reopening remains 'patchy and not fully transparent' (Vanda Insights via Reuters), so residual two-sided headline risk persists — directional read stays bearish for premia.

logistics.name: Gulf war-risk insurance / surcharges
logistics.note: War-risk premia continue to normalise as transit holds; keep cover in place while Iran contests maritime administrative control in case of re-escalation.

logistics.name: Land-bridge (Gulf → Jeddah / Red Sea)
logistics.note: Trucking workaround — retain as a live contingency while Gulf export volumes rebuild toward normal.

logistics.name: Incoterms / demurrage
logistics.note: Re-optimise CIF vs FOB exposure and keep demurrage clauses live until routing fully normalises.

inputs_summary: Track closely (material AND tradeable): power (largest cost — watch PPAs/carbon), calcined pet coke (rising, low transparency — the biggest carbon risk), silicon & magnesium (indexed, China-concentrated), copper (LME-transparent, ~$13,366/t).
Low transparency / contract (monitor via proxies): coal tar pitch, pre-baked anodes, cryolite, cathodes, Ti-B — no liquid index; track through supplier contracts and China export trends.
Highest supply-concentration risk: magnesium (~85% China), CPC (Asia / battery-anode competition), AlF₃ (fluorspar/China) — candidates to diversify or contract forward.

Logistics has flipped back to NORMAL: the Hormuz reopening (flows >10 mbpd) reverses the war-risk premium — normalise routing, keep cover on standby; BDI near a 2026-cycle low (~2,550) eases bulk costs.

Procurement read: power + carbon (coke/pitch) drive roughly half of conversion cost — prioritise these for hedging / forward contracting; alloying metals are smaller line items but China-concentrated.

long_term.label: Long-term — weekly (position degree)
long_term.writeup: Primary (weekly): the five-wave advance from the May-2025 low (~$2,300) topped at the $3,855 four-year high (early-Jun-2026) — a Hormuz-driven fifth-wave blow-off confirmed by bearish weekly RSI divergence — and an ABC correction is underway. Wave A bottomed at the 16-Jun $3,359 cycle low; wave B failed at the 22-Jun $3,406 official (intraday ~$3,607). Wave C is still extending but DECELERATING: the 01/02-Jul officials ($3,073/$3,065 3M) sit marginally below the .50 retrace ($3,077) with daily ranges compressing — consistent with a terminal C probing the $2,950–3,080 confluence (.50–.618 span / prior 4th-of-lesser-degree). Base case (~50%): C completes in this zone and a base forms; confirmation needs a reclaim of $3,260 (.382) and then a weekly close above the .236 ($3,488). Bear alternate ~30%: a sustained weekly close below $3,050 extends C to the .618 ($2,894). Bull ~20% only on a fast reclaim of $3,260/$3,488. The bearish-primary count is invalidated on a sustained weekly close above $3,666. Count unchanged from 02-Jul ('C extending'); no re-label. Confidence: medium.

short_term.label: Short-term — daily (swing degree)
short_term.writeup: Short-term (daily): the decline off $3,855 unfolded as wave A into the 16-Jun $3,359 low; wave B spiked ~6% intraday to ~$3,607 on 22-Jun (the contested Hormuz reclosure) and was rejected at the .50 retracement. Wave C has extended below the wave-A low but is DECELERATING: -1.25% (01-Jul) then -0.3% (02-Jul, $3,065 3M), with two closes marginally under the .50 ($3,077) and the curve flat — the classic signature of a maturing, possibly terminal, C leg. Alternation held (sharp A → sharp news-driven B). Base case: C completes in the $3,000–3,050 zone within 1–2 weeks and a base forms; the tape turns constructive on a reclaim of $3,260 (.382), firmer above $3,488. Invalidation of the basing view: consecutive daily closes below $3,030 open $2,950 and the .618 at $2,894. Count unchanged from the 30-Jun re-label ('C extending'). Confidence: medium — Friday's US payrolls is the near-term swing.

premium_drivers.d: Corridor risk keeps clearing: Hormuz crude flows >10 mbpd with UAE exports restored to >3.9 mbpd, and the market is pricing ~700kt of Gulf aluminium returning in H2-2026 — the premium's war component is nearly unwound, leaving a residual floor from still-impaired Gulf capacity (~30–60% run-rates into Q4).

50% tariff on full customs value (proclamation adjusted 1-Jun-2026, eff. 8-Jun-2026 through 2027; Annex I-C derivative carve-outs at lower temporary rates do not change the unwrought 50%) → US Midwest premiums remain structurally elevated (~$0.20–0.21/lb market premium + tariff) even as the LME falls.
EU import duty at 6% (reverted from 4% on 1-Jan-2026) plus CBAM carbon costs → European premiums structurally supported despite soft demand.

Gulf war-risk surcharges keep easing as transit normalises; dry-bulk cost direction turned up — BDI +3.4% to 2,650 on 02-Jul (capesize +6.2%) on Australia/Brazil ore activity — a modest offset in delivered-premium economics.

so_what.line: Aluminium is attempting to base at the $3,077 (.50 retracement): Friday's tape stabilised (spot reference ~$3,095, +0.3%) after a soft US June payrolls print (+57k) knocked the dollar to ~100.8 and trimmed Fed-hike odds — the first supportive macro impulse since the correction began.
points: The slide has stalled at support: the latest official close (02-Jul: $3,061.50 cash / $3,065 3M) was only -0.3% after -1.25% on 01-Jul, and Friday's spot reference ticked UP ~0.3% to ~$3,095 (Trading Economics) — price is hovering at, not breaking, the $3,077 (.50) level. Friday's officials publish next session.

Macro turned supportive: June payrolls +57k vs ~110k expected with -74k of back revisions (BLS) — DXY fell to ~100.8, September hike odds dropped to ~50% from ~64%, and gold's fix jumped to $4,064. A softer dollar removes one of the two active bear drivers.

Supply-side bear case is unchanged: Hormuz transit restored (crude >10 mbpd, UAE >3.9 mbpd), ~700kt of Gulf metal priced to return in H2, and China output/exports still rising — the rest of the base-metals board (Ni -1.0%, Zn -1.3%, Pb -0.4% on 02-Jul officials) shows the reflation impulse is aluminium-specific, not broad.

The cushion holds: LME stock at 300,275 t (steady ~1,500 t/day draws, ~92% Russian, at the ~300kt days-of-cover threshold), China ingot destocking -40kt w/w (SMM), Gulf smelters still ~30–60% of capacity into Q4.

Watch next week: whether the $3,077 (.50) basing attempt is confirmed by closes back above it (reclaim of $3,260/.382 stabilises the chart; a sustained break opens $2,950/$2,894), the pending Q3 MJP settlement (~$275–320), and post-holiday US data for Fed-path follow-through.

exec_summary: The post-conflict correction extended but began to base. LME cash settled at $3,400.00/t on Friday 19 June, down 3.8% on the week from the prior Friday close of $3,536.00 and roughly 12% below the 2 June four-year high of $3,855. The week opened soft (cash $3,419.50 Monday), printed an intraweek low of $3,359.00 on Tuesday as the ceasefire narrative dominated, then stabilised in a $3,400-3,405 band through Wednesday-Friday. Aluminum still holds about +14% year-to-date and ~+33% year-on-year. Crude eased further as the Gulf war-risk premium deflated and a steady dollar (DXY ~100) kept dollar-priced metals on the back foot - but the back-half stabilisation, not the headline drop, is the week's signal.

Under the flat-price weakness, the physical curve quietly re-tightened. The cash-to-3M spread dipped into a ~$30/t contango on Tuesday as de-escalation peaked, then flipped back to a +$2/t backwardation by Friday, while LME stocks drew a further ~4,600 t to ~315,300 t - a multi-year low near 1.3 days of global output. The US-Iran framework was formalised (MOU signed 17 June; official ceremony in Switzerland 19 June) and Hormuz is reopening, but gradually: 25 vessels transited Thursday, the most since April yet far below the ~110/day pre-war norm, and physical metal returns in months not weeks (EGA flagship ~12 months to full capacity, ALBA partially offline). Net read: the war premium has largely unwound, but a persistent deficit and a slow restart are cushioning the downside - a two-sided, range-building market with sharp-rebound risk if the 60-day framework slips.

price_table.note: 19 Jun settlement; week low $3,359 (16 Jun), high $3,419.5 (15 Jun); -12% from 2 Jun peak $3,855
19 Jun official; tracks cash; back-half stabilisation in a $3,398-3,410 band
Dipped to ~-$30 contango (16 Jun) at peak de-escalation, flipped back to +$2 backwardation by 19 Jun - prompt tightness reasserting
19 Jun; -4,625 t on the week; multi-yr low ~1.3 days output; still drawing - deficit persists despite peace narrative
Q2 oversupply persists; energy relief from softer oil adds pressure; Platts/Fastmarkets - verify PRA
Q2 settled $350-353/t (11-yr high, +81% QoQ from Q1 $195); Q3 talks open but Hormuz reopening now caps upside

drivers: US-Iran framework formalised: MOU signed 17 June (Versailles, post-G7), official ceremony in Switzerland Friday 19 June; mediator Pakistan confirmed the US port blockade ceases immediately and Tehran reopens Hormuz - the dominant catalyst that has now largely unwound the conflict premium.

Hormuz reopening is gradual, not instant: 25 commercial vessels transited on Thursday (the most since April) versus a ~110/day pre-war norm; transit requires registration for 'safe passage' (toll-free through the 60-day window), so physical flows normalise over weeks-to-months, not days.

Physical tightness reasserted under the headline: the cash-3M curve flipped from a mid-week ~$30 contango back to a +$2 backwardation by Friday, and LME stocks drew to ~315 kt (~1.3 days output, multi-year low) - the deficit is cushioning the flat-price downside.

Slow supply restart underpins the floor: EGA flagship ~12 months to full capacity, ALBA partially offline; Gulf pre-war supply was ~9% of global / ~25% of non-Chinese output, so the lost tonnage returns slowly even with the Strait open.

Compounding bears cap the upside: rising China output toward the ~45 Mt cap, the Indonesian smelter ramp, RUSAL diverting metal from China to Japan, and Guinea bauxite controls as a modest upstream offset; softer crude and a steady dollar (DXY ~100) round out the macro headwind.

implications.text: the flat-price correction has matured into a base-building range and the curve has re-tightened into backwardation, so prompt material retains a scarcity bid; use the stabilisation to layer disciplined forward hedges on priced tonnes rather than chasing fixed-price sales lower, and preserve value-added premium capture (billet/slab/PFA). Keep optionality for a sharp rebound should the 60-day framework slip.

regional premiums remain elevated (MJP Q2 $350-353/t, an 11-year high; US Midwest firm on Section 232) but a confirmed, sustained Hormuz reopening is the principal downside risk into 2H. Prioritise locking favourable Q3 MJP settlements and renewal terms now, before normalisation and RUSAL's China-to-Japan diversion feed through to premiums.

softer ex-China alumina (~$298-300/t, Q2 oversupply) caps net-seller upside; with vessels resuming Hormuz transit, begin a measured unwind of contingency-routing and war-risk insurance premiums and re-optimise CIF vs FOB exposure - but keep committed-cargo vessel cover until shipowners validate sustained safe passage (transit is rebounding but still well below pre-war volumes).

hold OTIF for strategic accounts through the transition and track the credibility/mechanics of the 60-day window, Hormuz transit counts (the real normalisation tell), EGA/ALBA restart cadence, LME stock direction and the cash-3M curve (the real tightness tell), China export/cap enforcement and the Indonesian ramp, plus US CPI/PPI and the Fed for the dollar.

outlook: Hormuz transit normalisation - whether daily vessel counts keep climbing toward the ~110/day pre-war norm and shipowners validate sustained safe passage; a stall would re-introduce a supply premium.

The cash-3M curve and LME stocks - continued draws and a holding/steepening backwardation would confirm the deficit persists despite the peace narrative; a flip back to contango would signal the unwind resuming.
Physical restart cadence - EGA/ALBA repair milestones gate how fast Gulf tonnage actually returns; the gap between visible and invisible inventories remains the key tightness risk.

Macro and competing supply - the 60-day framework's durability (slippage is the primary upside-price risk), China output against the ~45 Mt cap, the Indonesian ramp, and US CPI/PPI and the Fed for USD direction.
weekly.sources: LME official cash/3-month settlements and warehouse stock via Westmetall (week of 15-19 June: cash $3,400.0, 3M $3,398.0, stock 315,300 t on 19 Jun); benchmark CFD price (~$3,400.85 on 19 Jun, ~+33% YoY), USD/DXY, energy and macro via Trading Economics; US-Iran MOU / Strait of Hormuz reopening, 17 June signing and 19 June Switzerland ceremony, and Thursday's 25-vessel transit via Al Jazeera, NBC News, PBS, Seatrade Maritime, CNN and the UK House of Commons Library (14-19 June); EGA/ALBA restart timelines and Gulf supply share via Trading Economics and Fastmarkets; MJP Q2 premium (~$350-353/t, +81% QoQ) and RUSAL China-to-Japan diversion via S&P Global Platts, Discovery Alert and AlCircle; alumina FOB Australia via Fastmarkets and S&P Global Platts.
weekly.caveat: Note: 19 June SHFE, alumina and physical-premium figures are estimates or most-recent public anchors; daily PRA assessments (Platts/Fastmarkets/Argus) and SHFE/SMM quotes sit behind licensed feeds and should be reconciled to the desk's licensed data before pricing. This brief is market intelligence, not trading advice.
